name: Tom Fitton
subject: Is Hunter going to jail?
body:
We filed a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) suit against three
federal agencies for details of the Biden agencies’ decision to
expand asset protection to large depositors in the Silicon Valley and
Signature Bank government takeovers

[WEEKLY UPDATE]

[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30216&F=T&drurl=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuanVkaWNpYWx3YXRjaC5vcmcvdmlkZW9zL2h1bnRlci1nb2luZy10by1qYWlsLz91dG1fc291cmNlPWRlcGxveWVyJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX2NvbnRlbnQ9QU1qY3dPRGs1TlRRJTNEJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj13ZWVrbHkrdXBkYXRlJnV0bV90ZXJtPW1lbWJlcnM=&hash=74518147d5d13bf383bbc0e86acb8e89c6373ccf40c118fc0baab50ad9a4a28d]

Judicial Watch Sues for Details of Biden Administration Bank Bailouts

We filed a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) suit against three
federal agencies for details of the Biden agencies’ decision to
expand asset protection to large depositors in the Silicon Valley and
Signature Bank government takeovers (_Judicial Watch Inc. v. U.S.
Department of the Treasury, et al_
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30217&F=T&drurl=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuanVkaWNpYWx3YXRjaC5vcmcvZG9jdW1lbnRzL2p3LXYtdHJlYXN1cnktZmRpYy1jb21wbGFpbnQtMDExNzQvXV8uXyhObz91dG1fc291cmNlPWRlcGxveWVyJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX2NvbnRlbnQ9QU1qY3dPRGs1TlRRJTNEJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj13ZWVrbHkrdXBkYXRlJnV0bV90ZXJtPW1lbWJlcnM=&hash=bdf8c52ba7edd5dad22ccc0e3139ceb3efbb844a6301469f0cbdcbfebcd77cd8.
1:23-cv-01174)).

The fact that the responsible Biden officials are hiding documents
about the issue suggests that they have something to hide.

We sued in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia
against the U.S. Department of the Treasury, the Federal Deposit
Insurance Corporation and the Board of Governors of the Federal
Reserve System after the agencies failed to respond to March 13 and
14, 2023, FOIA requests for:

* All records relating to the systemic risk exception granted to
Silicon Valley Bank. Such records include minutes, memoranda, and any
communications between the [agency] and employees and/or
representatives of Silicon Valley Bank.
* All records concerning the systemic risk exception granted to
Signature Bank. Such records include minutes, memoranda, and any
communications between the [agency] and employees and/or
representatives of Signature Bank.

Silicon Valley Bank in Santa Clara, CA, failed
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30196&F=T&drurl=aHR0cHM6Ly9tb25leS51c25ld3MuY29tL2ludmVzdGluZy9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDMtMTIvbmV3LXlvcmstc3RhdGUtcmVndWxhdG9ycy1jbG9zZS1zaWduYXR1cmUtYmFuaz91dG1fc291cmNlPWRlcGxveWVyJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX2NvbnRlbnQ9QU1qY3dPRGs1TlRRJTNEJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj13ZWVrbHkrdXBkYXRlJnV0bV90ZXJtPW1lbWJlcnM=&hash=19b0e5ce0a13be792e6f25a1cd9866fd95ec7efeac626999d0a3cf7d574d83fc]
on
March 10, 2023, and Signature Bank in New York City failed two days
later. On March 12 the Fed, FDIC, and Treasury Department, with the
approval of President Biden, announced
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30197&F=T&drurl=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZmVkZXJhbHJlc2VydmUuZ292L25ld3NldmVudHMvcHJlc3NyZWxlYXNlcy9tb25ldGFyeTIwMjMwMzEyYi5odG0/dXRtX3NvdXJjZT1kZXBsb3llciZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9jb250ZW50PUFNamN3T0RrNU5UUSUzRCZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249d2Vla2x5K3VwZGF0ZSZ1dG1fdGVybT1tZW1iZXJz&hash=6636c89c9f94d89afe1eb293b8d2c136edce1417c50054bc1d16e31d6128957a]
that
a “systemic risk exception” was being invoked for both banks.
This, they claimed, would fully protect all depositors.

The FDIC’s standard deposit insurance coverage limit
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30198&F=T&&hash=6c9c0b8799d18c6900df5ee4c8ff4dd3ac4a8a8b0edc79c83674cdd7c136c094.]
is
$250,000 per depositor, per FDIC-insured bank, per ownership category.
A systemic risk exception allows
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30199&F=T&&hash=1d7eb692e148f31c25b94cd6464a86c1870c2725765d07c2a7604b19718293a9]
the
FDIC, after multiple agency reviews, to protect uninsured depositors
over the $250,000 insured limit.

_The Hill_ reported
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30200&F=T&drurl=aHR0cHM6Ly90aGVoaWxsLmNvbS9idXNpbmVzcy9lY29ub215LzM5MTA1OTYtcmVndWxhdG9ycy1leWUtd2F5cy10by1pbnN1cmUtYWxsLWJhbmstZGVwb3NpdHMvP3V0bV9zb3VyY2U9ZGVwbG95ZXImdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fY29udGVudD1BTWpjd09EazVOVFElM0QmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPXdlZWtseSt1cGRhdGUmdXRtX3Rlcm09bWVtYmVycw==&hash=07632c5aff73325772ba1dbe27e2d695b25916fd664dd6a1cc3396eee4d4f6f8]
that
midsize banks are lobbying federal officials to guarantee all bank
deposits over the next two years and that small, community banks
“have spoken out against paying more to cover the failure of larger
banks such as SVB.” The article points out that “only around 10
percent of deposits in community banks are over the $250,000 limit,
compared to roughly 90 percent for Silicon Valley Bank,” according
to Cam Fine, former CEO of the Independent Community Bankers of
America.

_Fortune_ reported that the FDIC listed large companies that were
“in no real danger” of failure that it bailed out
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30201&F=T&&hash=01e55bd6c69c81e1a38f1d99e79220e8bee3605a56bba801bad3f1c12df3e0ae]
in
the wake of the Silicon Valley Bank collapse:

> The $1 billion that Sequoia, the firm famous for backing iconic
> companies including Apple, Google and WhatsApp, had at SVB made up a
> fraction of its $85 billion assets under management.
>
> Kanzhun, which had $902.9 million in deposits with SVB ... was
> heavily backed by Chinese giant Tencent before it went public on the
> Nasdaq in 2021, was among the largest Chinese companies to IPO in
> the US that year.

Sen. J.D. Vance (R-Ohio) said
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30202&F=T&drurl=aHR0cHM6Ly90aGVoaWxsLmNvbS9idXNpbmVzcy8zOTI0MDI1LWhvdXNlLWxhd21ha2Vycy1yb2FzdC1yZWd1bGF0b3JzLW92ZXItZmFpbGVkLWJhbmtzLz91dG1fc291cmNlPWRlcGxveWVyJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX2NvbnRlbnQ9QU1qY3dPRGs1TlRRJTNEJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj13ZWVrbHkrdXBkYXRlJnV0bV90ZXJtPW1lbWJlcnM=&hash=332e739ccdc0f2b46b2ae9c69c6b0fba7b8089599bd986724616eb0155fe3452]
during
a hearing of the Senate Banking Committee. “I think, you know, had
had [sic] deposits of over $3 billion, and I think Roku had deposits
of over $500 million, but there are a lot of people, a lot of firms in
Silicon Valley Bank that had deposits well over $1 million, over $5
million.”

Governor Gavin Newsom (D-CA) reportedly lobbied
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30203&F=T&&hash=9132941f1cfec183b733af442fb7a3ef064714d8e73290e927709503b5e5f392]
for
the bailout “after the failed bank began lobbying California’s
government and donating to Newsom’s wife.” Nancy Pelosi and other
Democrats were lobbied
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30204&F=T&drurl=aHR0cHM6Ly9wdWNrLm5ld3MvdGhlLXJvbi1jb253YXktYmFpbG91dC8/dXRtX3NvdXJjZT1kZXBsb3llciZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9jb250ZW50PUFNamN3T0RrNU5UUSUzRCZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249d2Vla2x5K3VwZGF0ZSZ1dG1fdGVybT1tZW1iZXJz&hash=4cc2e20beab7794d76b4e8e628d30ee9f344e98758f449811e859df07b752566]
for the bailout. Silicon
Valley was also a major supporter
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30205&F=T&drurl=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud2lyZWQuY29tL3N0b3J5L3NpbGljb24tdmFsbGV5LW9wZW5zLXdhbGxldC1qb2UtYmlkZW4vP3V0bV9zb3VyY2U9ZGVwbG95ZXImdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fY29udGVudD1BTWpjd09EazVOVFElM0QmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPXdlZWtseSt1cGRhdGUmdXRtX3Rlcm09bWVtYmVycw==&hash=96d9ff9320b194db22360062bae2e718d897f16ef2b2873d54671322f3a3a9ca]
of
President Biden in the 2020 election.

The bank’s board
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30206&F=T&drurl=aHR0cHM6Ly9ueXBvc3QuY29tLzIwMjMvMDMvMTQvb2JhbWEtYWlkZS1oaWxsYXJ5LWRvbm9ycy1pbXByb3YtYWN0b3ItbWVldC1zdmJzLWJvYXJkLz91dG1fc291cmNlPWRlcGxveWVyJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX2NvbnRlbnQ9QU1qY3dPRGs1TlRRJTNEJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj13ZWVrbHkrdXBkYXRlJnV0bV90ZXJtPW1lbWJlcnM=&hash=8a6c77ffa30707ceaf033c22830edc5c87ac7e611673cf8fef2a83f68227f49b]
reportedly
had many Democratic Party ties, including “a Hillary Clinton
mega-donor” as well as someone who “worked for President Barack
Obama before her own political career spectacularly failed.” A third
was a “prolific contributor to Democrats, including Nancy Pelosi —
who owns a Napa Valley vineyard just 15 minutes” away.

We have been instrumental in uncovering previous bank bailout
information.

In February 2011, we obtained FDIC records
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30207&F=T&&hash=3e96162621da2c5d849e4f6be1adbf12bdf94284e507886e83dd8ee4bebff697]
through
a FOIA lawsuit filed on behalf of former Federal Reserve and FDIC
employee Vern McKinley that pertained to the Citigroup
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30218&F=T&drurl=aHR0cDovL3d3dy5zY3JpYmQuY29tL2Z1bGxzY3JlZW4vNjk2MDk5MzE/YWNjZXNzX2tleT1rZXktMTI3cWU3MmE2NjhsZjJoMmQycWUmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1kZXBsb3llciZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9jb250ZW50PUFNamN3T0RrNU5UUSUzRCZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249d2Vla2x5K3VwZGF0ZSZ1dG1fdGVybT1tZW1iZXJz&hash=c045564fd023fcf65d6f00d20cf52b7cbbb7e88babe1b18df9eeb96931d91ecc]
and Bank
of America
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30219&F=T&drurl=aHR0cDovL3d3dy5zY3JpYmQuY29tL2Z1bGxzY3JlZW4vNjk2MDk5MTM/YWNjZXNzX2tleT1rZXktMWJ2b3kyMXlqY21mdHgxaDlieDImdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1kZXBsb3llciZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9jb250ZW50PUFNamN3T0RrNU5UUSUzRCZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249d2Vla2x5K3VwZGF0ZSZ1dG1fdGVybT1tZW1iZXJz&hash=1e9ed98ee153fcdf91a2e20b2d3f5a7f9d9c98111564e332b5ef3ea4001177b0]
bailouts,
as well as documents detailing the FDIC’s Temporary Liquidity
Guarantee Program
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30220&F=T&drurl=aHR0cDovL3d3dy5zY3JpYmQuY29tL2Z1bGxzY3JlZW4vNjk2MDk5MjY/YWNjZXNzX2tleT1rZXktMjR6ZHNjM3ZkZzRtYTMzNzloOHYmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1kZXBsb3llciZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9jb250ZW50PUFNamN3T0RrNU5UUSUzRCZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249d2Vla2x5K3VwZGF0ZSZ1dG1fdGVybT1tZW1iZXJz&hash=2d448ee0515fd24f83da89b4b28e3d54eb7f0b26fa14d37d0a0e61b483f0d1d3],
which guaranteed unsecured debt of private financial institutions and
provided them “full coverage of non-interest bearing [sic] deposit
transaction accounts, regardless of dollar amount.” Included were
unredacted minutes from FDIC Board meetings during which FDIC
officials and staff discussed the rationale for the bailouts which
centered on the “systemic risk” of allowing the two financial
institutions to fail.

In October 2011, we petitioned
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30211&F=T&&hash=de10f6cef1bf7f11d7e2ef2e28287bc2ffc2c5424cf08bc2d92ef616aa339b83]
the
U.S. Supreme Court, asking for a review of a lower court ruling
validating the Federal Reserve’s decision to withhold documents
about its $29 billion bailout of Bear Stearns.

In April 2012, McKinley and I co-authored an op-ed in _The Washington
Times_
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30215&F=T&drurl=aHR0cHM6Ly9tLndhc2hpbmd0b250aW1lcy5jb20vbmV3cy8yMDEyL2Fwci8xMS9iZXJuYW5rZXMtZmFpcnktdGFsZS1yZWNlc3Npb24tc3RvcnktZm9yLWtpZHMvP3V0bV9zb3VyY2U9ZGVwbG95ZXImdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fY29udGVudD1BTWpjd09EazVOVFElM0QmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPXdlZWtseSt1cGRhdGUmdXRtX3Rlcm09bWVtYmVycw==&hash=ec73f8b841c6c614020283a3d832c7b424c096cf4823030cbf1562704563fb8f]
titled
“Bernanke’s Fairy Tale Recession Story for Kids; Records Show Fed
Had No Coherent Strategy for Bank Bailouts,” which stated in part:

> It’s an oldie but a goodie for our Federal Reserve chairman. In
> one of his recent lectures at George Washington University (GWU),
> Ben S. Bernanke made the self-congratulatory assertion that the
> “forceful policy response” led by the Federal Reserve in 2008
> helped avoid a more serious economic downturn.
>
> This rhetoric is nothing new. Mr. Bernanke has made similar remarks
> in the past. As he confided in one interview, “I was not going to
> be the Federal Reserve chairman who presided over the second Great
> Depression.” It is clear that like Treasury Secretary Timothy F.
> Geithner, who recently trumpeted the fourth anniversary of his role
> in the Bear Stearns bailout, Mr. Bernanke is aggressively using the
> GWU lectures to shape his legacy before he steps down.

‘LGBTQ+ TRAILBLAZERS’ JOIN 9/11 RESCUERS, HELEN KELLER IN DEPT OF
LABOR HALL OF HONOR

Last year we told you how the Census Bureau was spending
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=27081&F=T&drurl=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuanVkaWNpYWx3YXRjaC5vcmcvY2Vuc3VzLWJ1cmVhdS1nZW5kZXItaWRlbnRpdHktcXVlc3Rpb25zLW9uLXN1cnZleXMvP3V0bV9zb3VyY2U9ZGVwbG95ZXImdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fY29udGVudD1BTWpjd09EazVOVFElM0QmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPXdlZWtseSt1cGRhdGUmdXRtX3Rlcm09bWVtYmVycw==&hash=4fd8adbd4bba4e5b9e9114a2aa23e77a18f4f1076df9f9edab19999aebbde394]
$10
million to research how best to add questions about sexual orientation
and gender identity on surveys. A few months ago, I commented on the
CIA celebrating
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30221&F=T&drurl=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZm94bmV3cy5jb20vcG9saXRpY3MvdHdpdHRlci1jaWEtcHJpZGUtY2VsZWJyYXRpbmctaGlzdG9yeS1sZ2J0cS1vZmZpY2Vycy13aWxkbHktZHlzdG9waWFuP3V0bV9zb3VyY2U9ZGVwbG95ZXImdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fY29udGVudD1BTWpjd09EazVOVFElM0QmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPXdlZWtseSt1cGRhdGUmdXRtX3Rlcm09bWVtYmVycw==&hash=539fd1509b2eda6b38ba30e64388636c433f85fba218ec3ebe8c0c03aa0f2fe5]
its
LGBTQ+ officers.

Now Biden’s Department of Labor has joined the fray, honoring three
people whose contributions to American labor are dubious at best.
Our _Corruption Chronicles_ blog has the details
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30222&F=T&drurl=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuanVkaWNpYWx3YXRjaC5vcmcvbGdidHEtdHJhaWxibGF6ZXJzL106P3V0bV9zb3VyY2U9ZGVwbG95ZXImdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fY29udGVudD1BTWpjd09EazVOVFElM0QmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPXdlZWtseSt1cGRhdGUmdXRtX3Rlcm09bWVtYmVycw==&hash=75f4110a84441078f09e1f2db186ab18cda2ad146b99c4aee16b166b9ae3a675

> The Biden administration is inducting a transgender woman and two
> gay men into the Department of Labor (DOL) Hall of Honor, describing
> them as “LGBTQ+ Trailblazers”
>
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30223&F=T&drurl=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZG9sLmdvdi9uZXdzcm9vbS9yZWxlYXNlcy9vc2VjL29zZWMyMDIzMDgzMT91dG1fc291cmNlPWRlcGxveWVyJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX2NvbnRlbnQ9QU1qY3dPRGs1TlRRJTNEJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj13ZWVrbHkrdXBkYXRlJnV0bV90ZXJtPW1lbWJlcnM=&hash=4d76414db8c315d16168c807714aec8444a4e790c7ddc4fb76062dffe1642382]
that
> helped advance workplace protections and equity. The trio will join
> the likes of the 9/11 rescue workers who “engaged in a heroic
> effort to rescue survivors of terrorist attacks,” President Ronald
> Reagan, United Farm Workers of America leader César Chávez and
> disability rights advocate Helen Keller. Last year’s inductees
> were the essential workers of the Coronavirus pandemic who showed up
> when everyone else stayed home. The DOL notes that they were
> disproportionately women and workers of color.
>
> “The Labor Hall of Honor recognizes individuals and groups whose
> distinctive contributions to the field of labor have enhanced the
> quality of life of millions – yesterday, today, and for
> generations to come,” the agency writes in its Hall of Honor
> Inductees page
>
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30224&F=T&drurl=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZG9sLmdvdi9nZW5lcmFsL2Fib3V0ZG9sL2hhbGxvZmhvbm9yL2luZHVjdGVlcz91dG1fc291cmNlPWRlcGxveWVyJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX2NvbnRlbnQ9QU1qY3dPRGs1TlRRJTNEJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj13ZWVrbHkrdXBkYXRlJnV0bV90ZXJtPW1lbWJlcnM=&hash=28179847b6ab2788f8775546badde06b1d2653861392ba50f42eb7766ecec952].
> “Nominees are considered on a rolling basis, and formal induction
> ceremonies are conducted at the U.S. Department of Labor in
> Washington, D.C.” Every inductee is listed along with a portrait
> and a brief description of what they did to deserve the honor. The
> first to be recognized in 1989 was Cyrus S. Ching, a labor union
> leader who was appointed director of the Federal Mediation and
> Conciliation Service by President Harry Truman. Ching “established
> a pattern of constructive cooperation with employees,” according
> to the DOL, which includes a quote from Ching calling collective
> bargaining one of the greatest cornerstones of our democratic
> institutions.
>
> Besides the LGBTQ+ trailblazers this year’s honorees include
> dozens of Thai garment workers discovered by federal agents in 1995
> at a sweatshop in the southern California city of El Monte, which is
> in Los Angeles County. The case sparked national outcry, according
> to the DOL, and inspired legislation against human trafficking and
> forced labor. “Through sheer determination and perseverance, the
> workers defied the odds and fought bravely for the freedom, rights
> and protections long denied to them,” the agency writes in this
> year’s Hall of Honor announcement. “Their case galvanized
> significant changes in U.S. labor and immigration law.” The Thais
> were allowed to stay in the U.S. and were provided with a path to
> citizenship. “The El Monte Thai garment workers serve as a lasting
> reminder of the importance of the Department of Labor’s mission to
> protect rights of all workers,” said the agency’s acting
> secretary, Julie Su, adding that their contributions to federal
> labor and immigration laws cannot be understated.
>
> After all, the DOL conducts the annual Hall of Honor induction to
> “recognize extraordinary individuals whose distinctive
> contributions to the field of labor elevated working conditions,
> wages and overall quality of life of America’s working
> families.” It remains unclear how the LGBTQ+ trailblazers met the
> criteria, but the agency is nevertheless honoring them. The
> transgender woman, Aimee Stephens, got fired and received a
> severance package after working six years at a Detroit funeral home
> as a man. When Stephens informed her employer that she was taking
> leave to undergo gender affirming surgery and would return as a
> woman in 2013, the owner terminated her, and the Obama Equal
> Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) sued the business for sex
> discrimination. In 2016 a federal court ruled in favor of the
> funeral home, but a federal appellate court reversed the decision
> after the EEOC appealed. In 2020 the Supreme Court ruled against the
> funeral home, which argued that federal protections against sex
> discrimination do not include gender identity or transgender status.
>
> The two gay men who will be honored this month along with the
> transgender woman also took their case to the Supreme Court. Donald
> Zarda was a skydiving instructor in Long Island, New York when he
> came out as gay to ease a female customer’s concern. The customer
> complained and Zarda got fired for misconduct. He sued his employer
> alleging that he got fired based on his sexual orientation. A
> federal court in New York ruled in favor of the employer in 2014
> and, although Zarda died later that year, his family appealed on his
> behalf, citing violation of the Civil Rights Act. The other DOL
> honoree, Gerald Bostock, claims he got fired as a child welfare
> advocate in a Georgia county after joining a gay recreational
> softball league in 2013. Last year the county approved a $825,000
> settlement
>
[https://myjw.pr.judicialwatch.org/link.php?AGENCY=jw&M=27089954&N=64973&L=30225&F=T&drurl=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubmJjbmV3cy5jb20vbmJjLW91dC9vdXQtbmV3cy9tYW4td29uLWdheS1yaWdodHMtY2FzZS1zY290dXMtYWdyZWVzLXNldHRsZW1lbnQtcmNuYTU1OTU5P3V0bV9zb3VyY2U9ZGVwbG95ZXImdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fY29udGVudD1BTWpjd09EazVOVFElM0QmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPXdlZWtseSt1cGRhdGUmdXRtX3Rlcm09bWVtYmVycw==&hash=cbac643d0d32d88775d11a7aa9ec40c8b67589c7fee1a2b19a554a219e4086ee]
to

clean_body: We filed a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) suit against three federal agencies for details of the Biden agencies’ decision to expand asset protection to large depositors in the Silicon Valley and Signature Bank government takeovers [WEEKLY UPDATE] [] Judicial Watch Sues for Details of Biden Administration Bank Bailouts We filed a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) suit against three federal agencies for details of the Biden agencies’ decision to expand asset protection to large depositors in the Silicon Valley and Signature Bank government takeovers (Judicial Watch Inc. v. U.S. Department of the Treasury, et al [. 1:23-cv-01174)). The fact that the responsible Biden officials are hiding documents about the issue suggests that they have something to hide. We sued in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia against the U.S. Department of the Treasury, the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation and the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System after the agencies failed to respond to March 13 and 14, 2023, FOIA requests for: All records relating to the systemic risk exception granted to Silicon Valley Bank. Such records include minutes, memoranda, and any communications between the [agency] and employees and/or representatives of Silicon Valley Bank. All records concerning the systemic risk exception granted to Signature Bank. Such records include minutes, memoranda, and any communications between the [agency] and employees and/or representatives of Signature Bank. Silicon Valley Bank in Santa Clara, CA, failed [] on March 10, 2023, and Signature Bank in New York City failed two days later. On March 12 the Fed, FDIC, and Treasury Department, with the approval of President Biden, announced [] that a “systemic risk exception” was being invoked for both banks. This, they claimed, would fully protect all depositors. The FDIC’s standard deposit insurance coverage limit [.] is $250,000 per depositor, per FDIC-insured bank, per ownership category. A systemic risk exception allows [] the FDIC, after multiple agency reviews, to protect uninsured depositors over the $250,000 insured limit. The Hill reported [] that midsize banks are lobbying federal officials to guarantee all bank deposits over the next two years and that small, community banks “have spoken out against paying more to cover the failure of larger banks such as SVB.” The article points out that “only around 10 percent of deposits in community banks are over the $250,000 limit, compared to roughly 90 percent for Silicon Valley Bank,” according to Cam Fine, former CEO of the Independent Community Bankers of America. Fortune reported that the FDIC listed large companies that were “in no real danger” of failure that it bailed out [] in the wake of the Silicon Valley Bank collapse: The $1 billion that Sequoia, the firm famous for backing iconic companies including Apple, Google and WhatsApp, had at SVB made up a fraction of its $85 billion assets under management. Kanzhun, which had $902.9 million in deposits with SVB ... was heavily backed by Chinese giant Tencent before it went public on the Nasdaq in 2021, was among the largest Chinese companies to IPO in the US that year. Sen. J.D. Vance (R-Ohio) said [] during a hearing of the Senate Banking Committee. “I think, you know, had had [sic] deposits of over $3 billion, and I think Roku had deposits of over $500 million, but there are a lot of people, a lot of firms in Silicon Valley Bank that had deposits well over $1 million, over $5 million.” Governor Gavin Newsom (D-CA) reportedly lobbied [] for the bailout “after the failed bank began lobbying California’s government and donating to Newsom’s wife.” Nancy Pelosi and other Democrats were lobbied [] for the bailout. Silicon Valley was also a major supporter [] of President Biden in the 2020 election. The bank’s board [] reportedly had many Democratic Party ties, including “a Hillary Clinton mega-donor” as well as someone who “worked for President Barack Obama before her own political career spectacularly failed.” A third was a “prolific contributor to Democrats, including Nancy Pelosi — who owns a Napa Valley vineyard just 15 minutes” away. We have been instrumental in uncovering previous bank bailout information. In February 2011, we obtained FDIC records [] through a FOIA lawsuit filed on behalf of former Federal Reserve and FDIC employee Vern McKinley that pertained to the Citigroup [] and Bank of America [] bailouts, as well as documents detailing the FDIC’s Temporary Liquidity Guarantee Program [], which guaranteed unsecured debt of private financial institutions and provided them “full coverage of non-interest bearing [sic] deposit transaction accounts, regardless of dollar amount.” Included were unredacted minutes from FDIC Board meetings during which FDIC officials and staff discussed the rationale for the bailouts which centered on the “systemic risk” of allowing the two financial institutions to fail. In October 2011, we petitioned [] the U.S. Supreme Court, asking for a review of a lower court ruling validating the Federal Reserve’s decision to withhold documents about its $29 billion bailout of Bear Stearns. In April 2012, McKinley and I co-authored an op-ed in The Washington Times [] titled “Bernanke’s Fairy Tale Recession Story for Kids; Records Show Fed Had No Coherent Strategy for Bank Bailouts,” which stated in part: It’s an oldie but a goodie for our Federal Reserve chairman. In one of his recent lectures at George Washington University (GWU), Ben S. Bernanke made the self-congratulatory assertion that the “forceful policy response” led by the Federal Reserve in 2008 helped avoid a more serious economic downturn. This rhetoric is nothing new. Mr. Bernanke has made similar remarks in the past. As he confided in one interview, “I was not going to be the Federal Reserve chairman who presided over the second Great Depression.” It is clear that like Treasury Secretary Timothy F. Geithner, who recently trumpeted the fourth anniversary of his role in the Bear Stearns bailout, Mr. Bernanke is aggressively using the GWU lectures to shape his legacy before he steps down. ‘LGBTQ+ TRAILBLAZERS’ JOIN 9/11 RESCUERS, HELEN KELLER IN DEPT OF LABOR HALL OF HONOR Last year we told you how the Census Bureau was spending [] $10 million to research how best to add questions about sexual orientation and gender identity on surveys. A few months ago, I commented on the CIA celebrating [] its LGBTQ+ officers. Now Biden’s Department of Labor has joined the fray, honoring three people whose contributions to American labor are dubious at best. Our Corruption Chronicles blog has the details [ The Biden administration is inducting a transgender woman and two gay men into the Department of Labor (DOL) Hall of Honor, describing them as “LGBTQ+ Trailblazers” [] that helped advance workplace protections and equity. The trio will join the likes of the 9/11 rescue workers who “engaged in a heroic effort to rescue survivors of terrorist attacks,” President Ronald Reagan, United Farm Workers of America leader César Chávez and disability rights advocate Helen Keller. Last year’s inductees were the essential workers of the Coronavirus pandemic who showed up when everyone else stayed home. The DOL notes that they were disproportionately women and workers of color. “The Labor Hall of Honor recognizes individuals and groups whose distinctive contributions to the field of labor have enhanced the quality of life of millions – yesterday, today, and for generations to come,” the agency writes in its Hall of Honor Inductees page []. “Nominees are considered on a rolling basis, and formal induction ceremonies are conducted at the U.S. Department of Labor in Washington, D.C.” Every inductee is listed along with a portrait and a brief description of what they did to deserve the honor. The first to be recognized in 1989 was Cyrus S. Ching, a labor union leader who was appointed director of the Federal Mediation and Conciliation Service by President Harry Truman. Ching “established a pattern of constructive cooperation with employees,” according to the DOL, which includes a quote from Ching calling collective bargaining one of the greatest cornerstones of our democratic institutions. Besides the LGBTQ+ trailblazers this year’s honorees include dozens of Thai garment workers discovered by federal agents in 1995 at a sweatshop in the southern California city of El Monte, which is in Los Angeles County. The case sparked national outcry, according to the DOL, and inspired legislation against human trafficking and forced labor. “Through sheer determination and perseverance, the workers defied the odds and fought bravely for the freedom, rights and protections long denied to them,” the agency writes in this year’s Hall of Honor announcement. “Their case galvanized significant changes in U.S. labor and immigration law.” The Thais were allowed to stay in the U.S. and were provided with a path to citizenship. “The El Monte Thai garment workers serve as a lasting reminder of the importance of the Department of Labor’s mission to protect rights of all workers,” said the agency’s acting secretary, Julie Su, adding that their contributions to federal labor and immigration laws cannot be understated. After all, the DOL conducts the annual Hall of Honor induction to “recognize extraordinary individuals whose distinctive contributions to the field of labor elevated working conditions, wages and overall quality of life of America’s working families.” It remains unclear how the LGBTQ+ trailblazers met the criteria, but the agency is nevertheless honoring them. The transgender woman, Aimee Stephens, got fired and received a severance package after working six years at a Detroit funeral home as a man. When Stephens informed her employer that she was taking leave to undergo gender affirming surgery and would return as a woman in 2013, the owner terminated her, and the Obama Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) sued the business for sex discrimination. In 2016 a federal court ruled in favor of the funeral home, but a federal appellate court reversed the decision after the EEOC appealed. In 2020 the Supreme Court ruled against the funeral home, which argued that federal protections against sex discrimination do not include gender identity or transgender status. The two gay men who will be honored this month along with the transgender woman also took their case to the Supreme Court. Donald Zarda was a skydiving instructor in Long Island, New York when he came out as gay to ease a female customer’s concern. The customer complained and Zarda got fired for misconduct. He sued his employer alleging that he got fired based on his sexual orientation. A federal court in New York ruled in favor of the employer in 2014 and, although Zarda died later that year, his family appealed on his behalf, citing violation of the Civil Rights Act. The other DOL honoree, Gerald Bostock, claims he got fired as a child welfare advocate in a Georgia county after joining a gay recreational softball league in 2013. Last year the county approved a $825,000 settlement [] to resolve the case. We just need one final push before midnight to ensure we show Andy's opponent and his super PAC allies that we have what it takes to win. [**Can we count on you? Join the hundreds of supporters who have chipped in today to help us before our filing deadline at midnight. Together, we can win this tough race and protect Kentucky's progress.**](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/69438732/426139856/-995335578?refcode=em23_230908_ddln8_fndd_gq_act___TB_ettx&utm_campaign=em23_230908_ddln8_fndd_gq_act___TB_ettx&utm_medium=email&refcode2=52cfdb9f-9f4e-ee11-a3f1-00224832e1ba&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMzEvMS85MzM0NiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI1MmNmZGI5Zi05ZjRlLWVlMTEtYTNmMS0wMDIyNDgzMmUxYmEiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=Ch9J1rjZ0qkcqYc9HLODhtHZhPHxX0x43SFLZGQa8FM=)

Election experts are predicting this will be the closest race in the entire country this November, and _Politico_ is calling the match-up between me and my opponent **"dramatically close."**

I'm not surprised, team. I won the race for governor in 2019 by just over 5,000 votes. We always knew this race would be just as close.

name: =?utf-8?Q?Michael=20Maher?=
subject: Pelosi's Big Announcement
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY MAHER FOR CONGRESS
body:
| | | [View this email in your browser](https://mailchi.mp/maherforcongress/opening-day-12767138?e=1722d05e10)
---
| | [ ](https://michaelmaher.us18.list-manage.com/track/click?u=6239b7d9cb3ad9388556be8be&id=8b241c595d&e=1722d05e10)
---
| | Dear Friend –

At 83 years old, Nancy Pelosi announced today that she is running for ANOTHER term in Congress. I certainly know 80 year olds can do the job, but that isn't the point. Stay with me on this crucial distinction.

Nancy Pelosi has been in Congress since 1987–Ronald Reagan was President, George Deukmejian was Governor. My opponent, Jim Costa, has been in Congress since 2005–Arnold Schwarzenegger was Governor, George W. Bush was President. The world has changed a lot since both Pelosi and Costa were first elected.

My point is that it isn't just time for new blood in Congress for the sake of new faces. It's important to our future to elect people with fresh perspectives, ideas, and have the energy to make America all that it can be.

As someone who just stepped into my 4th decade, I believe that my experience as a United States Navy Nuclear Submarine Veteran, law enforcement agent, small business owner, and now dad to young kids has equipped me to serve and inspires me to help chart a positive and prosperous path for our country.

Join with me as I fight this election battle and work to place our country on a positive trajectory.

Please commit $5 per week to help build a better America.
---
| [$5 A WEEK](https://michaelmaher.us18.list-manage.com/track/click?u=6239b7d9cb3ad9388556be8be&id=745711bb4b&e=1722d05e10 "$5 A WEEK")
---
| Or you can make a difference in our campaign with a one-time donation of any amount.
---
| [ONE-TIME DONATION](https://michaelmaher.us18.list-manage.com/track/click?u=6239b7d9cb3ad9388556be8be&id=c98f0a12a6&e=1722d05e10 "ONE-TIME DONATION")
---
| Either way, your help will ensure that we can win, move forward, and keep a Republican majority in Congress, rather than go backward with more of the same from Costa & Pelosi.

Sincerely,
---
|
---
| **Michael Maher**
Businessman | Veteran | Republican for Congress

---
|
---
| _**Background** : Michael Maher is a former FBI Special Agent and US Navy Veteran who was raised in the Central Valley. Michael and his wife, Ann, are raising their young family in Fresno County, and he owns and operates an aviation business. Michael was the 2022 nominee for California's 21st Congressional District._
---

name: Jayapal HQ
subject: We cannot let racism win
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Pramila for Congress
body:
The truth is: if we don’t stop it, Trump could very well be the next GOP nominee for president.
#
Peter,
With dark-money organizations like “No Labels” working to sway our election in favor of Trump’s campaign and steal support away from President Biden — and wealthy mega-donors funneling millions into Trump’s pockets — **we need to do everything we can to protect our democracy.**
As we move towards the 2024 elections, we’re sure to see even more pro-Trump supporters and legislators do everything they can to silence our progressive base to push their far-right agenda.
Our progressive movement pulled out a win in 2020 and stopped the red wave in 2022 — and we’re ready to do it all over again with you on our side. **[If you stand with our progressive base and want to see even more leaders like Pramila elected in 2024, chip in today to strengthen our campaign so that we can keep up this fight.](https://act.pramilaforcongress.com/go/45511?refcode=em20230908-2899&t=1&akid=2899%2E420207%2EQQDtFN)**
**[Donate ››](https://act.pramilaforcongress.com/go/45511?refcode=em20230908-2899&t=20&akid=2899%2E420207%2EQQDtFN)**
Trump’s presidential run was the catalyst to unleashing a wave of hateful and harmful legislation on our country. And it exposed a budding fascist movement that would stop at nothing to halt our progress — even inciting a coup attempt.
While Trump might finally be held accountable for his actions and role in inciting an insurrection — and more — he is still the leading Republican contender in the 2024 presidential race.
**The truth is: Donald Trump could very well be the next GOP nominee for president.**
We cannot let racism, xenophobia and transphobia win, Peter. **[If you are able, please consider contributing $3 to our campaign today to help re-elect Pramila and continue growing our grassroots movement to defeat the far-right.](https://act.pramilaforcongress.com/go/45511?refcode=em20230908-2899&t=21&akid=2899%2E420207%2EQQDtFN)**
Together,
Jayapal HQ

**[Contribute](https://secure.actblue.com/donate/pj-email-footer?refcode=em20230908-2899-footer&t=22&refcode2=2899_420207_QQDtFN&akid=2899%2E420207%2EQQDtFN)**
---
[](https://www.facebook.com/pramila.jayapal) | [](https://twitter.com/pramilajayapal) | [](https://www.instagram.com/pramilajayapal/)
Email us: info@pramilaforcongress.com
This email was sent to dpwillis67@gmail.com. Email is the most important way we keep in touch with people like you, and small-dollar contributions sustain our movement for transformative change. To contribute by check, please address to Pramila for Congress, PO Box 21912, Seattle, WA 98111.

* [Update My Personal Information](https://act.pramilaforcongress.com/signup/did-your-info-change/?t=23&akid=2899%2E420207%2EQQDtFN)
* [Unsubscribe](https://act.pramilaforcongress.com/unsubscribe/unsubscribe/?t=24&akid=2899%2E420207%2EQQDtFN)
